preFix.title: Chocolate-dipped shortbread fingers
preFix.subtitle: Scottish shortbread biscuits half-dipped in dark chocolate
preFix.excerpt: Plain shortbread fingers dipped halfway in melted dark chocolate and left to set. The biscuit underneath is a standard butter shortbread with a high proportion of butter for a short, sandy crumb. The dark chocolate provides a contrast in both flavour and texture.
preFix.sourceNotes: Shortbread is documented in Scottish and British baking literature from the eighteenth century. Chocolate-dipped shortbread fingers are a well-established British confectionery-bakery combination. The recipe here follows standard shortbread practice.

content.text: The shortbread base is plain butter shortbread: flour, icing sugar, butter, and salt, baked until just pale. Once cool, each finger is half-dipped in dark chocolate. Full tempering is not needed at home;
by melting carefully and cooling to 32 degrees C before use gives a good result.

items.name: Flaky sea salt
items.prepNote: a very small pinch per finger, for finishing

content.text: Preheat the oven to 160 degrees C (conventional). Beat the butter and icing sugar until just combined; do not cream until pale. Add the salt. Mix in the flour until the dough comes together. Press into a lightly floured surface and roll to about 8 mm thick. Cut into fingers roughly 3 cm wide and 10 cm long. Transfer to a lined baking tray. Bake for 16 to 18 minutes until very lightly coloured at the edges. Leave to cool on the tray for 5 minutes, then transfer carefully to a rack. Leave to cool completely; shortbread is fragile when warm.

Melt the dark chocolate in a heatproof bowl set over a pan of barely simmering water, stirring gently. Remove from the heat once fully melted and leave to cool to about 32 degrees C, stirring occasionally; the bowl should feel just below body temperature. Dip each cooled shortbread finger halfway into the chocolate, letting the excess drip off, then lay it on a sheet of baking paper. Sprinkle a tiny pinch of flaked salt over the wet chocolate if you like. Leave at room temperature until set, at least 30 minutes; do not refrigerate, as this causes condensation on the chocolate surface.
